---
title: "Swiss TPH Powerpoint presentation example"
author: "Research IT"
format: pptx
editor: source
metadata-files:
  - _ppt_metadata.yml
---

<!--
================================================================================
GUIDE TO THE YAML HEADER ABOVE  (this block is a comment; it does NOT render)
================================================================================

SAFE TO CUSTOMISE — change these freely:

  title:    Presentation title shown on the title slide. Any text.
  author:   Presenter name shown on the title slide. Any text.
  editor:   Only affects the RStudio editing experience (visual vs source).
            Has no effect on the rendered .pptx. Safe to change or remove.

CHANGE ONLY IF YOU KNOW WHAT YOU ARE DOING — these can break rendering:

  format: pptx
            Selects the PowerPoint output format and the Quarto machinery
            behind it. Changing the format value re-routes everything; do not
            edit unless you intend to switch output type entirely.

  metadata-files:
    - _ppt_metadata.yml
            Pulls in extra settings from the companion file _ppt_metadata.yml
            (see below). Keep this line and keep _ppt_metadata.yml in the SAME
            working directory as this .qmd, or rendering loses the template and
            the execute options.

SETTINGS LIVING IN _ppt_metadata.yml (edit there, not here) — handle with care:

  reference-doc: ppt_template2.pptx
            The PowerPoint template providing the Swiss TPH slide layouts
            (Title Slide, Title and Content, Section Header, Two Content,
            Comparison, Content with Caption, Blank). The .pptx must sit in
            this working directory. Replacing it with a file that lacks these
            named layouts will break or mis-style slides. Do NOT rename or move.

  execute: { echo: false, warning: false }
            Hides R code and warnings in the output. Setting echo: true dumps
            all R source onto the slides — usually not what you want for a
            presentation.

NOTE ON CONTENT STRUCTURE (not YAML, but it controls slides):
  Level-1 headings (#) create SECTION slides; level-2 headings (##) create
  CONTENT slides. This is the slide-level convention the template expects —
  keep the # / ## pattern below intact.
================================================================================
-->

# Introduction to Quarto

## Quarto

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished presentation. To learn more about Quarto presentations see <https://quarto.org/docs/presentations/>.

## Bullets

When you click the **Render** button a document will be generated that includes:

-   Content authored with markdown
-   Output from executable code

# Classic Layout

## Agenda

|                |
|----------------|
| Agenda item #1 |
| Agenda item #2 |
| Agenda item #3 |
| Agenda item #4 |
| Agenda item #5 |
| Agenda item #6 |

## Title and Text

Title and subtitle should only be one line each.

Insert here to highlight the first point you would like to address.

Short and sweet is more effective.

## Two Contents

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
Put your content here. Put your content here. Put your content here.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
Put your content here. Put your content here. Put your content here.
:::
:::::

## Text with Image

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
-   Insert text here to go along with your photo selection on the right.

-   Try to keep bullet point content concise, clear, and relating to the subject of the photo.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
![Image1](./images/pptx_example_image1.jpg)
:::
:::::

## Slide with Speaker Notes

Put your content here. Put your content here.

::: notes
Speaker notes go here.
:::

# Graphics generated by R

## Colors

```{r}
# Load Swiss TPH graphical chart colors
stph_colors <- stphtoolbox::stph_palette()
scales::show_col(stph_colors)
```

## Boxplot

The iris dataset was used for this demo.

```{r}
library(dplyr)
iris %>% 
  stphtoolbox::vertical_boxplot("Sepal.Length",
                                "Species")
```

## Bar chart

The iris dataset was used for this demo.

```{r}
library(ggplot2)
iris %>%
  ggplot2::ggplot(aes(x = Species, y = Sepal.Length, fill = Species)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "summary", fun = "mean") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = stph_colors) +
  theme_minimal()
```

## Pie chart

The iris dataset was used for this demo.

```{r}
as.data.frame(table(iris$Species)) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = "", y = Freq, fill = Var1)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", width = 1) +
  coord_polar("y", start = 0) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = stph_colors) +
  theme_void()
```
